items.title: The safe learning loop pipeline
items.description: The safe learning loop is the ordered pipeline the platform runs to learn from work: observe the source, tests, and reports; extract code-graph facts; mine candidate code blocks; search matching knowledge; optionally ask a model for a suggestion; run deterministic validation; create knowledge proposals; create code-block proposals; store experience; update the benchmark; and recommend a generator-template improvement. Every output is a proposal, not an applied change.
items.applicability: Run after any completed task so learning is captured systematically rather than ad hoc.
items.example: After a feature passes its tests, the loop extracts the new call edges, mines a candidate block, finds related knowledge, and emits proposals plus an experience entry, applying nothing on its own.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: implement each stage as a discrete step that produces proposals, never direct mutations. Test implication: assert that running the loop creates proposals and experience but changes no production artefact. Generator implication: template improvements are recommendations awaiting approval. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Observe source, tests, and reports as evidence
items.description: Learning begins by observing concrete evidence: the source that changed, the tests that ran, and the validation reports they produced. The platform treats these artefacts as the ground truth from which any later proposal is derived, so nothing is learned from assumption alone.
items.applicability: The first stage of the learning loop, gating everything downstream on real observed outcomes.
items.example: The loop reads the modified files, the passing and failing test names, and the build and Playwright reports before forming any candidate insight.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: capture evidence as durable references (paths, test ids, report ids), not as a summary that loses traceability. Test implication: assert each downstream proposal links back to a specific observed artefact. Generator implication: generators learn only from observed runs, not imagined ones. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Extract graph facts and mine code blocks
items.description: From observed source the platform extracts structured code-graph facts (new nodes and edges) and mines candidate code blocks (recurring shapes that solved the problem). These become the raw material for proposals, turning a one-off change into reusable, queryable knowledge.
items.applicability: The extraction stage that converts observed code into graph updates and block candidates.
items.example: A new posting service yields graph edges to the GL writer and a candidate accounting-posting block summarising its inputs, files, and validation rules.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep extracted facts as proposals to the graph and block store, reviewable before they are trusted. Test implication: assert a known new relationship is extracted and a recurring shape is mined as a candidate. Generator implication: only approved blocks become generator targets. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Search matching knowledge before proposing new
items.description: Before creating a knowledge proposal the platform searches existing approved knowledge for a match, so it reinforces or refines what is already known rather than duplicating it. Matching is lexical, comparing titles, tags, and text, and is treated as a hint, not a certainty.
items.applicability: The deduplication gate between mining a candidate and proposing new knowledge.
items.example: A mined insight about anti-forgery on POST forms matches an existing auth-hardening item, so the loop proposes a refinement rather than a duplicate.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: run the search before proposing and attach any near-matches to the proposal for the reviewer. Test implication: assert a candidate that overlaps existing knowledge is flagged as a potential duplicate. Generator implication: generators prefer reinforcing existing items. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic so true synonyms can be missed, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Optional model suggestion, never authority
items.description: A model may optionally suggest a candidate insight, block refinement, or template change, but its suggestion enters the loop as a proposal subject to the same validation and approval as any other. The model proposes and reviews; deterministic validation and a human decide.
items.applicability: The optional suggestion stage, used only when a model is available and never as a gate.
items.example: A model proposes generalising two similar blocks into one; the proposal is recorded with the model as its origin and held for validation and approval.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: label model-originated proposals as such and route them through the standard gates, never directly to the store. Test implication: assert a model suggestion alone cannot approve or apply anything. Generator implication: generators act on approved proposals regardless of origin. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Deterministic validation is the authority
items.description: Within the learning loop, the repeatable build, test, security, and Playwright gates are the authority on whether observed work succeeded and whether a derived block is sound. No model verdict or confidence score can override a failing gate, and no proposal claims success without one.
items.applicability: The validation stage that separates trustworthy evidence from mere suggestion across the whole loop.
items.example: A mined block whose generated form fails the build gate is recorded as a failure proposal, not a usable block, regardless of how plausible it looked.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute proposal status from gate results, not from model output. Test implication: assert a block that fails any gate cannot be proposed as ready. Generator implication: only gate-passing blocks are offered for reuse. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Create knowledge proposals, never auto-approve
items.description: Insights the loop derives are written as knowledge proposals in a pending state. They are not injected into the curated, retrieve-first memory until a human approves them, preserving the integrity of the approved knowledge base.
items.applicability: The knowledge-output stage of the loop and the boundary of the approval lifecycle.
items.example: A new rule about isolating import failures is saved as a pending proposal; until approved it does not appear in prompt context as approved knowledge.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep pending proposals in a separate state that retrieval treats as unapproved. Test implication: assert a pending proposal is not returned as approved knowledge and cannot self-approve. Generator implication: generators use only approved knowledge. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Create code-block proposals with evidence
items.description: Mined code blocks are saved as proposals, each linked to the source, tests, and validation report that justify it. A block proposal is only as trustworthy as its evidence, so the link is mandatory and reviewable.
items.applicability: The block-output stage of the loop, feeding the reusable-block store after approval.
items.example: A proposed report-endpoint block carries links to the file it was mined from and the passing test that proves its shape, ready for a reviewer to accept or reject.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: reject a block proposal that lacks an evidence link. Test implication: assert every block proposal references at least one source artefact and one validation result. Generator implication: only approved, evidenced blocks become templates. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Store experience and update the benchmark
items.description: Each loop run stores an experience entry (what was attempted, the outcome, and the evidence) and updates a benchmark of how the platform is performing over time. Experience guides future planning and the benchmark makes progress measurable rather than asserted.
items.applicability: The memory and measurement stage at the end of the loop.
items.example: A successful import feature adds an experience entry and nudges the benchmark for import-related tasks upward; a failure records the cause for next time.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: record both successes and failures so the benchmark is not biased by hidden failures. Test implication: assert a failed run still produces an experience entry and a benchmark update. Generator implication: generators weight choices by benchmarked success. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Recommend generator-template improvements
items.description: When the loop sees a template repeatedly produce work that needs the same manual fix, it recommends improving that template. The recommendation is a proposal a human reviews; templates are not rewritten automatically.
items.applicability: The improvement stage that closes the loop by feeding lessons back into generation.
items.example: If generated CRUD lists repeatedly need a heavy column removed, the loop proposes updating the CRUD template to exclude such columns by default.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: base recommendations on repeated evidence, not a single instance, and keep them as proposals. Test implication: assert a recommendation references the recurring failures that motivated it. Generator implication: a template changes only after its improvement proposal is approved. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Require human approval before any real mutation
items.description: No proposal of any kind (knowledge, block, graph fact, or template change) takes effect until a human approves it. The loop can prepare, evidence, and stage change, but the act of applying it is reserved for an authorised person.
items.applicability: The governing rule over every output the learning loop produces.
items.example: A fully validated, well-evidenced block proposal still sits in pending state until a reviewer approves it, at which point it joins the reusable store.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: make approval a distinct, audited human action with no automated shortcut. Test implication: assert no pending proposal can transition to applied without an approval record. Generator implication: generators consume only approved artefacts. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Learn from passing and failing tests
items.description: Both outcomes teach. A passing test confirms a block shape and raises confidence in the pattern that produced it; a failing test reveals a gap and yields a proposal describing the failure mode to avoid. The loop mines insight from green and red alike.
items.applicability: Whenever the loop observes a test result, regardless of whether it passed.
items.example: A passing posting test reinforces the double-entry block; a failing one because debits did not equal credits proposes a guard against unbalanced input.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive a distinct proposal type for failures versus confirmations. Test implication: assert a failing test produces a failure-mode proposal and a passing one raises a confirmation. Generator implication: generators encode learned failure modes as validation rules. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Learn from build failures and Playwright failures
items.description: A build failure or a failed Playwright flow is high-value evidence of a structural or behavioural defect. The loop captures the failure, its likely cause, and a proposal to prevent it, so the same break is less likely to recur in generated work.
items.applicability: Whenever a build or end-to-end gate fails during observed work.
items.example: A Playwright proof that fails because a form lacks anti-forgery yields a proposal to make the secure-login template always emit the token.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: capture the failing gate and the specific symptom, not just a pass/fail flag. Test implication: assert a build or Playwright failure produces a linked, actionable proposal. Generator implication: generators turn recurring failures into default validation rules. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Learn from bug fixes, generated products, and official-doc extraction
items.description: The loop also learns from a completed bug fix (the defect and its remedy), from a generated product (which blocks composed well), and from extracting durable facts out of official documentation. Each source yields proposals that broaden the platform's grounded knowledge.
items.applicability: Applied opportunistically whenever any of these richer evidence sources is available.
items.example: A bug fix for a race in stock valuation proposes a serialisation rule; reading an official decimal-precision doc proposes a fixed-precision storage rule.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: tag the origin of each proposal so reviewers can weigh it, and summarise official docs originally rather than copying them. Test implication: assert a bug-fix observation produces a remedy proposal linked to the fix. Generator implication: generators reuse the broadened knowledge once approved. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Avoid duplicates and reject unsafe proposals
items.description: Two safeguards protect the knowledge base: the loop flags likely duplicates so approved memory stays clean, and it rejects proposals that are unsafe (for example, one that would weaken a security rule or apply a destructive change) before they ever reach a reviewer as acceptable.
items.applicability: The quality and safety filter applied to every proposal the loop produces.
items.example: A proposal that suggests skipping anti-forgery to simplify a form is auto-rejected as unsafe; a near-identical knowledge item is flagged as a duplicate rather than added.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: encode unsafe patterns as hard rejections and surface duplicate candidates to the reviewer. Test implication: assert an unsafe proposal is rejected and a duplicate is flagged. Generator implication: generators never receive rejected or duplicate proposals. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic so subtle duplicates can slip through, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Never self-modify the main repository
items.description: The learning loop never edits the platform's own main repository. It produces proposals and stores them in its knowledge and block stores; changing the running system's code is exclusively a human, reviewed, version-controlled act. The model proposes and reviews only.
items.applicability: An absolute boundary on what the loop and any agent may do to the platform itself.
items.example: Even after discovering an obvious improvement to its own generator, the loop records a recommendation; it does not commit a change to the repository.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: deny the loop and agent runner any write access to the main repo and route everything through proposals. Test implication: assert the loop cannot modify a repository file under any path. Generator implication: generated products are separate artefacts, never edits to the platform. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.

items.title: Compose blocks into a feature plan and report gaps and maturity delta
items.description: To build a feature the platform composes approved blocks into a plan, mapping each requirement to a block. Where no block fits, it reports the missing block honestly rather than improvising silently, and it measures the maturity delta the work produced (which rungs were reached, what knowledge grew).
items.applicability: The composition discipline used when turning a request into a buildable, honestly-scoped plan.
items.example: A request for a supplier portal composes the login, CRUD, and audit blocks, reports that no payment-reconciliation block exists yet, and records the resulting maturity gain.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: represent a feature plan as a block map with explicit gaps, and compute the delta from gate results. Test implication: assert a plan lists unmatched requirements as missing blocks and reports a measured delta. Generator implication: generators build only the matched blocks and flag the gaps for a human. Honest boundary: proposals need human approval, matching is lexical not semantic, and there is no autonomous mutation.
